summary: DeVault argues that while machine learning technology is real and here to stay, the AI bubble will pop and leave behind a world that is crappier, not better. He dismisses AGI fears as overblown and contends that AI's real impact will be replacing competent humans with cheap, mediocre automation. The lasting effects will include degraded creative work, overwhelmed digital spaces, more convincing scams, and elimination of human customer support. He frames the AI hype as a deliberate propaganda campaign by 'AI barons' to secure investment and lobby for regulatory moats, while the real threat comes not from sentient machines but from capitalists and militaries deploying AI tools that discriminate and harm.
key_points: Contemporary ML advances aren't approaching AGI — the real impact is boring capitalism replacing expensive humans with cheap, mediocre automation
The lasting effects of AI will be overwhelmingly negative: content farms, propaganda, eliminated jobs, degraded creative work, and flooded markets
AI companies externalize massive costs onto digital infrastructure and exploit cheap labor markets for data tagging
The sci-fi mystique around AI is deliberate propaganda designed to help investors lobby for regulatory moats around their industry
The actual danger of AI is not sentience but its deployment by militaries, police, and courts in ways that discriminate against minorities and scale human malice
money_quotes: There is a machine learning bubble, but the technology is here to stay. Once the bubble pops, the world will be changed by machine learning. But it will probably be crappier, not better.
Its staying power will come in the form of replacing competent, expensive humans with crappy, cheap robots.
The future of media is a soulless, vapid regurgitation of all media that came before the AI epoch, and the fate of all new creative media is to be subsumed into the roiling pile of math.

To the public, the present-day and potential future capabilities of the technology are played up in breathless promises of ridiculous possibility. In closed-room meetings, much more realistic promises are made of cutting payroll budgets in half.

The technology is nowhere near this level, a fact well-known by experts and the barons themselves, but the illusion is maintained in the interests of lobbying lawmakers to help the barons erect a moat around their new industry.
You will never trust another product review. You will never speak to a human being at your ISP again.

AI is defined by aggressive capitalism. The hype bubble has been engineered by investors and capitalists dumping money into it, and the returns they expect on that investment are going to come out of your pocket.
The singularity is not coming, but the most realistic promises of AI are going to make the world worse.
key_insight: AI's real threat is not sentient machines but aggressive capitalism replacing quality with cheap automation while engineering hype to secure regulatory advantages.
